message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 12 (29 todo)
Recent changes: Added curseSurchargeFor helper adding 50% of cursed items' base premium; added to basePremiumFor.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Note: upcoming tests add a high-enchantment (>=5) surcharge of 30% per item, so consider whether item-specific modifiers could share structure.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 12 tests pass. Now I'll work through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The recent Green change added `curseSurchargeFor`. Let me evaluate the names:

- `curseSurchargeFor(items)` — clearly reveals intent: computes the curse surcharge for a set of items. Good.
- `basePremiumFor`, `priceGroup`, `countByType`, `BASE_PREMIUMS` — all clear and accurate.

**Decision: Keep current names.** They reveal intent accurately.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (the recently-changed code)

I'll focus on the area changed in Green: `curseSurchargeFor` plus its integration into `basePremiumFor`.

```
function curseSurchargeFor(items: QuoteItem[]): number {
  let surcharge = 0;                                    // assignment(6) + const 0 (1) + binding surcharge(1)
  for (const item of items) {                           // loop(5) + binding item(1) + binding items(1)
    if (item.cursed) {                                  // conditional(4) + invocation .cursed access? treat as binding(1)
      surcharge += BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type] * 0.5;       // assignment(6) + invocation index(2) + invocation *(2) + const 0.5(1) + binding(1)
    }
  }
  return surcharge;                                     // binding(1)
}
```

Approximate mass of `curseSurchargeFor`: ~33.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 12 green. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names are clear.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** I see emerging structural duplication. The `0.5` curse multiplier is an item-specific modifier applied per-item against `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]`. The hint tells me the next test adds a *high-enchantment surcharge of 30% per item* — also an item-specific, per-item percentage of the base premium. There's a strong shared shape: "for each item, sum a percentage of its base premium when a condition holds."

However, the refactor discipline says: refactor based on duplication that **currently exists**, not speculative future needs (Rule 4: fewest elements — don't add premature abstraction). Right now there is only **one** item-specific modifier, so abstracting a generic "modifier" mechanism now would be premature and add elements without current justification. The hint asks me to *consider* shared structure — and the honest assessment is that the right moment to extract it is when the second modifier arrives (triangulation), not before.

There IS one small concrete improvement available now that genuinely reduces duplication and improves clarity without premature abstraction: the magic number `0.5` is an unexplained literal. Naming it as a constant (like the other premiums) reveals intent.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unnecessary abstraction to remove. Avoid adding one prematurely.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll extract the curse multiplier magic number into a named constant, matching the existing style (`BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_PREMIUM`, `PROCESSING_FEE`). This reveals intent (Rule 2) for the `0.5` literal.

content.text:
All 12 tests stay green.

## Step 5: APP Mass Change

The change replaced a magic constant literal `0.5` with a named binding `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`. The added line `const CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5;` adds one assignment/binding + one constant (+~2 mass). At the use site, `0.5` (constant, 1) becomes a binding reference (1) — net neutral there. Overall mass change: 33 → ~35 (Δ +2). This is the expected, accepted trade-off: Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP. The slight mass increase buys a named, self-documenting rate that matches the file's existing constant style.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted magic number `0.5` into named constant `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, matching the existing `BLOCK_SIZE` / `BLOCK_PREMIUM` / `PROCESSING_FEE` style.

**Considered but deliberately deferred:**
- A generic per-item modifier abstraction to share structure with the upcoming 30% high-enchantment surcharge (per the hint). With only **one** item-specific modifier existing today, extracting that abstraction now would be premature (violates Rule 4 — fewest elements — and adds speculative structure). The right trigger is the second modifier arriving in the next tests; at that point `curseSurchargeFor` and the new enchantment surcharge will share an obvious "sum a percentage of each qualifying item's base premium" shape that can be extracted via triangulation. The current code is already well-positioned for that: both will be per-item functions returning a surcharge that `basePremiumFor` sums in.

## Step 7: Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted magic number `0.5` into named constant `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (Rule 2 — reveals intent).
- **Mass Change**: ~33 → ~35 (Δ +2); accepted trade-off, clarity trumps APP.
- **Deferred**: Shared item-modifier abstraction — postponed to next test (avoid premature abstraction; will extract via triangulation when the enchantment surcharge lands).
- **Tests**: All 12 passing ✅ (29 todo).

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_06-12-03_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test.
